document_metadata.title: Introspection of Grief After a Year: Cope How You Can
document_metadata.first_words: By Kristin Dudley It’s officially been a year since the death of my dad and it has not come without its challenges. I’m middle-aged, unmarried, and without the distraction of children and all the busy things that go along with that. I’ve stayed around to help my mom, which as two adults in a parent-child living situation, has probably not been the best for either of us. I’ve not coped. I am still certain he’s just away on a long trip. The part that doesn’t kick in is that it’s a one-way ticket, and his soul is gone

content_metadata.document_title_txt: Introspection of Grief After a Year: Cope How You Can

chunks.content_original:
What I really hate about my selfishness is that there have been other people who have passed this year whom I loved and respected or were within my dad’s family. I lost a great-aunt and great-uncle this year, too. I couldn’t find the strength to say goodbye to them. I should’ve been there for my cousins when they lost their grandpa. I wasn’t. I should’ve been there to say goodbye to a teacher I had in high school that I thought a lot of. I couldn’t. I don’t know what it is, but I’ve silently hidden myself from other avenues of grief. The daily aspects were enough.

In reality, it wasn’t just grief. I have recognized that I sank into a deep dark depression and it’s been one that almost took me with it completely. But as a society— we don’t talk about that.

So I am.

I’m telling you some songs help me get through. Highly recommend ol Jellyroll, “I Am Not Okay.” It helped me to normalize the feeling. For anyone unfamiliar, the lyric goes:

‘I am not OK. I’m barely gettin by.

I’m losing track of days. And losing sleep at night.

chunks.content_original:
**By Kristin Dudley**

It’s officially been a year since the death of my dad and it has not come without its challenges. I’m middle-aged, unmarried, and without the distraction of children and all the busy things that go along with that. I’ve stayed around to help my mom, which as two adults in a parent-child living situation, has probably not been the best for either of us.

I’ve not coped. I am still certain he’s just away on a long trip. The part that doesn’t kick in is that it’s a one-way ticket, and his soul is gone from this earthly world. I have fought for peace, which seems like a paradox in itself.

Death is weird and comes with its strange array of feelings. I have felt completely deregulated emotionally for a year now. It seems like everyone else handles their grief better or can tuck it away into a box so as to not feel it. I wish I could, but my therapist won’t let me. I’m grateful for those who have still checked in periodically, and I’m disappointed in the relationships that have broken along the way.

chunks.content_original:
If I can help one person rise out of the ashes, the pain has been brutal, but it will have been worth it to put myself out there.

Please– look at ways to heal and cope. Try not to take on the coping skills alone and do’t let someone else project their coping onto you. SPeaking from experience– not healthy!

Do it your own way. Not what’s best for someone else (which is how I tried to). And consider doing it with help. GriefShare, support groups, therapy. You might not be OK, but you’re also not alone.
chunks.content:

chunks.content_original:
What’s strange about losing someone so close to you is the way that it consumes you. Or maybe I am using ‘you’ as a phrase rather than ‘I’ so that I don’t have to take ownership of that feeling. Some seem to be untethered by the sadness they carry. I wish I were like that. Unfortunately, grief has consumed me. Grief has been pumping through my veins stronger than blood for a year now, and the pain is more horrific than a thousand shards of glass.

Some days are filled with rage. Some with feelings of abandonment. Some with gratitude, joy, and love. I felt really bad for people who had lost a parent. But there is truth in the statement, ‘you don’t know until you go through it.’
chunks.content:
What’s strange about losing someone so close to you is the way that it consumes you. Or maybe I am using ‘you’ as a phrase rather than ‘I’ so that I don’t have to take ownership of that feeling. Some seem to be untethered by the sadness they carry. I wish I were like that. Unfortunately, grief has consumed me. Grief has been pumping through my veins stronger than blood for a year now, and the pain is more horrific than a thousand shards of glass.

Some days are filled with rage. Some with feelings of abandonment. Some with gratitude, joy, and love. I felt really bad for people who had lost a parent. But there is truth in the statement, ‘you don’t know until you go through it.’

chunks.content_original:
But it’s been a year. A struggle of a year.

And I know there’s people out there fighting the same fight, hoping for inner peace.

It doesn’t just come on the holidays either. It comes every single day of the week.

So– I use this as a platform to encourage you to deal with grief how you want to, and though a program might outline a way to cope healthily, that it may not be quite time to start it, and that’s OK. Some of us need help getting through the tough stuff. I’m speaking up about my battle.

My own personal sadness has consumed me. It has made me say things I didn’t mean. It has filled me with rage and anger. It has turned me into someone who used to be a joyful little pepster with all the positivity in the world into someone ready to throat punch anyone happy.

It’s time to move past some of that. It’s time to get my little hamster brain off the wheel and actually make my dad proud. It’s time to stop hiding out, and this is basically a proclamation, putting all of myself out there in hopes that it might reach at least one person.
chunks.content:

It’s time to move past some of that. It’s time to get my little hamster brain off the wheel and actually make my dad proud. It’s time to stop hiding out, and this is basically a proclamation, putting all of myself out there in hopes that it might reach at least one person.

chunks.content_original:
I’m losing track of days. And losing sleep at night.

I am not OK.

I’m hangin on the rails

So if I say I’m fine, just know that I’ve learned to hide it well.’

It goes on to say, “I know I can’t be the only one who’s holdin on for dear life. But God knows, I know when it’s all said and done, I’m not OK, but it’s all gonna be alright.”

I want to thank the human in my life who sent that song to me and told me it helped them through a tough time. Because when I felt alone in my sadness, I knew I probably wasn’t the only one in the world going through a tough time.

There’s pictures I’ve looked at for hours. I’ve scanned for every voicemail, every email, every video…. just for another glimpse or sound of my dad. The nighttime in silence when the world is quiet, that’s when the grief becomes deafening.

The tears fall, the anger kicks in and there’s despair in looking for a sign.

But it’s been a year. A struggle of a year.

chunks.content_original: As a card-carrying holder of the dead dads club, I was at first appalled when someone used that phrase but I realized that humor might just be their coping skill, and now I get it too. There is instantly a bond and connection to someone who has lost a parent. A friend in high school had lost both of her parents by the time she was a junior in high school. And just this past week, I know there were some young children, one around two months old, who lost their daddy too. It hurts, and somehow their strength is mountains more than my own. I kind of want to hold those babies and tell them, they’ll make it, but it would be more for my own healing, speaking those words aloud, than it would be for theirs.
chunks.content: As a card-carrying holder of the dead dads club, I was at first appalled when someone used that phrase but I realized that humor might just be their coping skill, and now I get it too. There is instantly a bond and connection to someone who has lost a parent. A friend in high school had lost both of her parents by the time she was a junior in high school. And just this past week, I know there were some young children, one around two months old, who lost their daddy too. It hurts, and somehow their strength is mountains more than my own. I kind of want to hold those babies and tell them, they’ll make it, but it would be more for my own healing, speaking those words aloud, than it would be for theirs.

chunks.content_original:
When I first started the process, I realized I was the only one in my family who was going to attempt to cope through counseling. I was invited to a grief seminar and I probably should have gone but I just wasn’t ready. I also tried to participate in an online version of the GriefShare support group. I attended once and realized I was still not ready. I knew I wouldn’t talk around people I knew so I went online so that I could be a stranger. Still– not ready. What’s fantastic about GriefShare is that there is some sort of a dialog based on different topics, hands-down the only guidebook for grief I’ve ever seen.

Truth is..there’s no guidebook. If you’re being a jerk, you get a little grace. Lord knows I’ve asked for some. Eleven months ago I was about as selfish as they came, and that’s the only way I knew how to deal. I didn’t say I was coping, and I would say that how I chose to deal with it wasn’t probably the healthiest.

raw_content:
{ "id" : 101716, "date" : "2025-12-27T02:39:22", "date_gmt" : "2025-12-27T02:39:22", "modified_gmt" : "2025-12-27T02:39:23", "link" : "https://www.chillicothenews.com/introspection-of-grief-after-a-year-cope-how-you-can/", "title" : { "rendered" : "Introspection of Grief After a Year: Cope How You Can" }, "content" : { "rendered" : "

By Kristin Dudley

It’s officially been a year since the death of my dad and it has not come without its challenges. I’m middle-aged, unmarried, and without the distraction of children and all the busy things that go along with that. I’ve stayed around to help my mom, which as two adults in a parent-child living situation, has probably not been the best for either of us.

I’ve not coped. I am still certain he’s just away on a long trip. The part that doesn’t kick in is that it’s a one-way ticket, and his soul is gone from this earthly world. I have fought for peace, which seems like a paradox in itself.

Death is weird and comes with its strange array of feelings. I have felt completely deregulated emotionally for a year now. It seems like everyone else handles their grief better or can tuck it away into a box so as to not feel it. I wish I could, but my therapist won’t let me. I’m grateful for those who have still checked in periodically, and I’m disappointed in the relationships that have broken along the way.

What’s strange about losing someone so close to you is the way that it consumes you. Or maybe I am using ‘you’ as a phrase rather than ‘I’ so that I don’t have to take ownership of that feeling. Some seem to be untethered by the sadness they carry. I wish I were like that. Unfortunately, grief has consumed me. Grief has been pumping through my veins stronger than blood for a year now, and the pain is more horrific than a thousand shards of glass.

Some days are filled with rage. Some with feelings of abandonment. Some with gratitude, joy, and love. I felt really bad for people who had lost a parent. But there is truth in the statement, ‘you don’t know until you go through it.’

As a card-carrying holder of the dead dads club, I was at first appalled when someone used that phrase but I realized that humor might just be their coping skill, and now I get it too. There is instantly a bond and connection to someone who has lost a parent. A friend in high school had lost both of her parents by the time she was a junior in high school. And just this past week, I know there were some young children, one around two months old, who lost their daddy too. It hurts, and somehow their strength is mountains more than my own. I kind of want to hold those babies and tell them, they’ll make it, but it would be more for my own healing, speaking those words aloud, than it would be for theirs.

When I first started the process, I realized I was the only one in my family who was going to attempt to cope through counseling. I was invited to a grief seminar and I probably should have gone but I just wasn’t ready. I also tried to participate in an online version of the GriefShare support group. I attended once and realized I was still not ready. I knew I wouldn’t talk around people I knew so I went online so that I could be a stranger. Still– not ready. What’s fantastic about GriefShare is that there is some sort of a dialog based on different topics, hands-down the only guidebook for grief I’ve ever seen.

Truth is..there’s no guidebook. If you’re being a jerk, you get a little grace. Lord knows I’ve asked for some. Eleven months ago I was about as selfish as they came, and that’s the only way I knew how to deal. I didn’t say I was coping, and I would say that how I chose to deal with it wasn’t probably the healthiest.

What I really hate about my selfishness is that there have been other people who have passed this year whom I loved and respected or were within my dad’s family. I lost a great-aunt and great-uncle this year, too. I couldn’t find the strength to say goodbye to them. I should’ve been there for my cousins when they lost their grandpa. I wasn’t. I should’ve been there to say goodbye to a teacher I had in high school that I thought a lot of. I couldn’t. I don’t know what it is, but I’ve silently hidden myself from other avenues of grief. The daily aspects were enough.

In reality, it wasn’t just grief. I have recognized that I sank into a deep dark depression and it’s been one that almost took me with it completely. But as a society— we don’t talk about that.

So I am.

I’m telling you some songs help me get through. Highly recommend ol Jellyroll, “I Am Not Okay.” It helped me to normalize the feeling. For anyone unfamiliar, the lyric goes:

‘I am not OK. I’m barely gettin by.

I’m losing track of days. And losing sleep at night.

I am not OK.

I’m hangin on the rails

So if I say I’m fine, just know that I’ve learned to hide it well.’

It goes on to say, “I know I can’t be the only one who’s holdin on for dear life. But God knows, I know when it’s all said and done, I’m not OK, but it’s all gonna be alright.”

I want to thank the human in my life who sent that song to me and told me it helped them through a tough time. Because when I felt alone in my sadness, I knew I probably wasn’t the only one in the world going through a tough time.

There’s pictures I’ve looked at for hours. I’ve scanned for every voicemail, every email, every video…. just for another glimpse or sound of my dad. The nighttime in silence when the world is quiet, that’s when the grief becomes deafening.

The tears fall, the anger kicks in and there’s despair in looking for a sign.

But it’s been a year. A struggle of a year.

And I know there’s people out there fighting the same fight, hoping for inner peace.

It doesn’t just come on the holidays either. It comes every single day of the week.

So– I use this as a platform to encourage you to deal with grief how you want to, and though a program might outline a way to cope healthily, that it may not be quite time to start it, and that’s OK. Some of us need help getting through the tough stuff. I’m speaking up about my battle.

My own personal sadness has consumed me. It has made me say things I didn’t mean. It has filled me with rage and anger. It has turned me into someone who used to be a joyful little pepster with all the positivity in the world into someone ready to throat punch anyone happy.

It’s time to move past some of that. It’s time to get my little hamster brain off the wheel and actually make my dad proud. It’s time to stop hiding out, and this is basically a proclamation, putting all of myself out there in hopes that it might reach at least one person.

If I can help one person rise out of the ashes, the pain has been brutal, but it will have been worth it to put myself out there.

Please– look at ways to heal and cope. Try not to take on the coping skills alone and do’t let someone else project their coping onto you. SPeaking from experience– not healthy!

Do it your own way. Not what’s best for someone else (which is how I tried to). And consider doing it with help. GriefShare, support groups, therapy. You might not be OK, but you’re also not alone.

markdown_content:
**By Kristin Dudley**

It’s officially been a year since the death of my dad and it has not come without its challenges. I’m middle-aged, unmarried, and without the distraction of children and all the busy things that go along with that. I’ve stayed around to help my mom, which as two adults in a parent-child living situation, has probably not been the best for either of us.

I’ve not coped. I am still certain he’s just away on a long trip. The part that doesn’t kick in is that it’s a one-way ticket, and his soul is gone from this earthly world. I have fought for peace, which seems like a paradox in itself.

Death is weird and comes with its strange array of feelings. I have felt completely deregulated emotionally for a year now. It seems like everyone else handles their grief better or can tuck it away into a box so as to not feel it. I wish I could, but my therapist won’t let me. I’m grateful for those who have still checked in periodically, and I’m disappointed in the relationships that have broken along the way.

What’s strange about losing someone so close to you is the way that it consumes you. Or maybe I am using ‘you’ as a phrase rather than ‘I’ so that I don’t have to take ownership of that feeling. Some seem to be untethered by the sadness they carry. I wish I were like that. Unfortunately, grief has consumed me. Grief has been pumping through my veins stronger than blood for a year now, and the pain is more horrific than a thousand shards of glass.

Some days are filled with rage. Some with feelings of abandonment. Some with gratitude, joy, and love. I felt really bad for people who had lost a parent. But there is truth in the statement, ‘you don’t know until you go through it.’

As a card-carrying holder of the dead dads club, I was at first appalled when someone used that phrase but I realized that humor might just be their coping skill, and now I get it too. I kind of want to hold those babies and tell them, they’ll make it, but it would be more for my own healing, speaking those words aloud, than it would be for theirs.

Eleven months ago I was about as selfish as they came, and that’s the only way I knew how to deal. I didn’t say I was coping, and I would say that how I chose to deal with it wasn’t probably the healthiest.

What I really hate about my selfishness is that there have been other people who have passed this year whom I loved and respected or were within my dad’s family. I lost a great-aunt and great-uncle this year, too. I couldn’t find the strength to say goodbye to them. I should’ve been there for my cousins when they lost their grandpa. I wasn’t. The daily aspects were enough.

In reality, it wasn’t just grief. I have recognized that I sank into a deep dark depression and it’s been one that almost took me with it completely. But as a society— we don’t talk about that.

So I am.

I’m telling you some songs help me get through. Highly recommend ol Jellyroll, “I Am Not Okay.” It helped me to normalize the feeling. For anyone unfamiliar, the lyric goes:

‘I am not OK. I’m barely gettin by.

I’m losing track of days. And losing sleep at night.

I am not OK.

I’m hangin on the rails

So if I say I’m fine, just know that I’ve learned to hide it well.’

It goes on to say, “I know I can’t be the only one who’s holdin on for dear life. But God knows, I know when it’s all said and done, I’m not OK, but it’s all gonna be alright.”

I want to thank the human in my life who sent that song to me and told me it helped them through a tough time. Because when I felt alone in my sadness, I knew I probably wasn’t the only one in the world going through a tough time.

There’s pictures I’ve looked at for hours. I’ve scanned for every voicemail, every email, every video…. just for another glimpse or sound of my dad. The nighttime in silence when the world is quiet, that’s when the grief becomes deafening.

The tears fall, the anger kicks in and there’s despair in looking for a sign.

But it’s been a year. A struggle of a year.

And I know there’s people out there fighting the same fight, hoping for inner peace.

It doesn’t just come on the holidays either. It comes every single day of the week.

My own personal sadness has consumed me. It has made me say things I didn’t mean. It has filled me with rage and anger. It has turned me into someone who used to be a joyful little pepster with all the positivity in the world into someone ready to throat punch anyone happy.

It’s time to move past some of that. It’s time to get my little hamster brain off the wheel and actually make my dad proud. It’s time to stop hiding out, and this is basically a proclamation, putting all of myself out there in hopes that it might reach at least one person.

If I can help one person rise out of the ashes, the pain has been brutal, but it will have been worth it to put myself out there.

Please– look at ways to heal and cope. Try not to take on the coping skills alone and do’t let someone else project their coping onto you. SPeaking from experience– not healthy!

Do it your own way. Not what’s best for someone else (which is how I tried to). And consider doing it with help. GriefShare, support groups, therapy. You might not be OK, but you’re also not alone.
